data.title: Germany: The Corn's About To Pop
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

Hopes of an early German recovery were quickly shelved

Hopes for an end to German stagnation in 2025 had to be quickly shelved. After the U-turn on fiscal stimulus and the new government coalition agreement, optimism returned to the country, only for it to enter a new phase of national depression in the second half of the year, driven by doubts about creative budget accounting and a clear lack of structural reforms. Since the end of 2022, there have been only two quarters in which the economy actually grew.

Still, this dreadful economic performance had at least one upside: at the end of the year, everyone seems to have finally understood that Germany’s economic problems are not just a fatal concatenation of unfortunate circumstances but rather the result of years of underinvestment, a lack of structural reforms and the rise of China from export destination to system rival. US tariffs and the stronger euro only aggravated the problems, but they are not the root cause.

Time to get more positive about Germany

At first glance, the past year gave little reason for optimism, but rather sent the entire country into national depression. There are good reasons to finally be more positive about the German economy. The latest macro data indicates a clear turning point in industry. Industrial orders have now increased for three consecutive months, and even the argument that the November surge was driven by bulk orders does not really concern us; with the fiscal spending programme, more of these bulk orders will come this year.

By 2026, bulk orders could be the new normal, not the exception. Which brings us to the main reason for optimism: fiscal stimulus. The announced infrastructure and defence investment plans will finally begin to reach the economy this year. Critics often overlook the sluggishness of Germany’s federal decision-making process. It took until late last year for parliament to approve the 2026 budget and almost 30 military procurement contracts. With the rapid expansion of defence production capacity, there is a good chance that a large part of the defence spending will remain within the domestic economy and not leak to other countries.

In fact, we see the defence sector as a potential positive surprise in 2026. Last but not least, even though corporates don’t get tired of complaining about elevated energy costs, the government’s decision to bring those costs down to one-third of their current levels should bring further relief, if it's implemented.

Private consumption will lag behind

While industry should stage a decent rebound, private consumption will remain muted in 2026. Over the last four years, German unemployment has increased by some 500,000 people. This gradual worsening reflects textbook economics: with the economy effectively stagnating for more than five years and industry facing severe structural challenges, a deterioration in the labour market was inevitable. This deterioration appears set to continue, as the number of corporate insolvencies has not yet declined. With ongoing uncertainty about the future of Germany’s pension system and anticipated higher costs associated with demographic change, it remains difficult to foresee a significant pickup in private consumption in the near term.

The three Rs: from rebound to recovery... only with reforms

An economic liftoff is clearly in the making, and we expect some 1% GDP growth for this year. However, the economy’s problems are deeply rooted, often structural and largely self-made, except for the China problem. Solving these issues quickly is impossible. This is a completely different challenge from some twenty years ago, when Germany had also been the ‘sick man of Europe’.

This time around, the economy almost needs a complete makeover, ranging from well-known measures such as reducing red tape and introducing e-government to mastering and lowering the financial burden of demographics or through tax cuts. It is up to German Chancellor Friedrich Merz and his government to implement these reforms this year and turn a long-awaited rebound into a sustainable recovery.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Prospect Capital Is The Last Remaining BDC Bond Opportunity With A +8% Yield
data.description:
A year ago, we started a series looking for opportunities with yields of around 8% in the world of BDCs. At the beginning, we found and described quite a few such cases, but recently they have become increasingly rare. At the moment, the only such example in the BDC universe is the OTC bond of Prospect Capital (PSEC), which currently offers a yield to maturity of over 8%. Therefore, today we will focus in more detail on this case.

PSEC Basic Information

  • Total assets (Q3 2025): around $6.6 billion
  • Current market capitalization: approximately $1.3 billion
  • Expenses:

PSEC has a total leverage of close to 53.38%. Its expense ratio is around 14%, the non-leverage expense ratio is close to 5.95%, and the gross-asset non-leverage expense ratio is around 2.69%. The 1-year z-score is a negative 0.78, and the 3-year z-score is a negative 1.62.

  • Holdings: The company has close to 92 investments across 32 industries
  • Portfolio:

85% of the portfolio is in senior and senior secured investments (71% first lien). Around 63% of the assets are unencumbered.

One of the biggest parts of the portfolio is 14% in the "Real Estate" sector, 12% in the "Health Care Providers & Services" sector, and 11% in the "Consumer Finance" sector. More information can be seen in the picture below.

  • Total returns:

The 10-year total return on NAV of PSEC is close to 4.27%, and the 5-year total return on NAV is around 1%.

  • Common stock:

The common stock is currently trading at $2.78, with a close to 57% discount.

  • Historical Performance:

And here is shown a historical 10-year premium/discount chart - you can see how the stock is traded at a significant discount most of the time:

  • Credit ratings:

PSEC has a "BB+" credit rating from S&P, cut from "BBB-" in 2024, due to the realization of the company's big losses, cutting dividends, and others. The preferred stock rating comes to "B+" from "BB". More details below:

Preferred stock

PSEC has one preferred stock at the time of writing:

(PSEC.PR.A) was issued on 07/12/2021, with a coupon rate of 5.25%. The spread to the treasury yield was 3.42%, while currently it is around 3.05%. It has close to 0.51 years to call, with a call date on 07/19/2026. Its current yield is close to 7.84%. But we are more interested in the company's bonds, so let's see them.

OTC bonds

Shown above are PSEC's over-the-counter bonds. Currently, the highest-yielding bond has a maturity date of 15/10/2028 and a yield to maturity above 8.1%. Moody's rates it "Ba1," and its yield to worst is the highest among both OTC BDC bonds and Exchange Traded BDC Debt.

BDC OTC universe:

BDC exchange-traded debt:

Credibility

Now, we would like to provide you with a more detailed credit rating calculation based on Moody's methodology to assess the company's strengths and weaknesses.

  • Profitability: Here, we get a 3-year average Net Income/Assets of around-2%, which corresponds to a credit rating of Caa3.
  • Leverage (Asset Coverage Ratio)- Baa3: The regulatory minimum for BDCs is 150%. PSEC's asset coverage ratio cushion is close to 18%, with an asset coverage ratio of 177%:

As of September 30, 2025, our asset coverage ratio stood at 339.0% based on the outstanding principal amount of our senior securities representing indebtedness of $1,935,415 and our asset coverage ratio on our senior securities that are stock was 177.3%.

  • Asset Quality-Problem loans are close to 0.7%, which is equivalent to an A3 credit rating:

Close to 71% of assets are senior secured, which is a Baa3 rating score.

  • Debt Structure Debt distributions: Baa3.

    The secured debt is around 10% of the assets, which equates to an A2 rating.

Final Rating: The weighted average credit score corresponds to "Baa3". But when we adjust that score with a discount to NAV, the historically high IPO yields of the company's bonds, and recent problems in the underlying portfolio, we get an overall adjusted credit score of "B1". We see such a large difference between the two scores mainly because of the significant discount to the company's NAV. The market-adjusted asset coverage ratio, for example, is well below the regulatory limit. The final score is definitely below investment grade, both by the rating agencies and by our adjustments:

Conclusion

With limited yield opportunities in the BDC universe of 8%, the PSEC OTC'28 bond stands out with an attractive yield to maturity of over 8%. However, this yield reflects an average credit rating. As a result, the bond offers a higher yield opportunity for investors who are willing to accept increased risk in exchange for a better YTM.

data.title: NewtekOne: Only One Baby Bond Yields Over 8%
data.description:
Introduction

NewtekOne (NEWT) is a depositor bank that transitioned from being a business development company three years ago. In addition to common shares, the bank has a preferred share (NEWTP) that is yielding 9.1%. Additionally, there are four different baby bond offerings. Back in September, I wrote in support of the bank's longest dated baby bond (NEWTH). Today, I still support investing in the bank's baby bonds, but investors should direct any new money towards NEWTG, which is currently yielding the highest of the group.

NewTekOne Earnings

Lower interest rates have affected NewTekOne's business. Asset yields declined by nearly 50 basis points in the third quarter to 7.68%. This is the lowest asset yield level since the company started reporting loan performance metrics in 2023. Fortunately, the bank's borrowing yields also fell to their lowest level, dropping 44 basis points in the third quarter to 5.34%. Unfortunately, asset yields have fallen faster than borrowing yields, so net interest spread has fallen for two consecutive quarters and net interest margin is down 20 basis points on the quarter at 2.86%. Net interest margin is currently at its lowest level since the second quarter of 2024 (2.81%).

Despite the compression in interest rates, Newtek is still growing its interest income, which increased by $2 million in the third quarter to $39 million. Interest expenses also increased by $2 million to $25 million. Net interest income (interest income less interest expense) edged up slightly and hit the $15 million level in the third quarter for the first time.

Changes in Loans and Deposits

NewtekOne is still transitioning away from the legacy assets of its BDC days into a depositor bank. Because of this, lending and deposits are both on a growth rip but will eventually mature when the transition is completed. Loans grew by nearly 18% in the third quarter and are 58% higher than where they were a year ago. Deposits increased by just under 9% for the quarter and are up 81% on a year over year basis. These growth levels are above and beyond what the average bank experiences.

One area of concern is the bank's loan-to-deposit ratio, which represents the percentage of deposits used to fund lending. Because of the transition, NewtekOne's loan to deposit ratio was abnormally high in late 2023 and early 2024 at over 200%. Over the last few quarters, there's been an inconsistent decline, but the ratio remains high at 160%.

A high loan to deposit ratio means that NewtekOne is dependent upon external forms of financing to fund loan growth. External financing is more expensive for the bank than deposits, but investors should not be surprised at the current levels since the company was entirely externally financed as a BDC. External financing did rise in the third quarter to $749 million, but it rose at a slower rate than lending and now represents less than 40% of gross loans for the first time.

Risks to NewtekOne

NewtekOne is in a fragile position with the high loan to deposit ratio, therefore the existing loan performance problems are an exacerbated risk to the bank. Nonperforming assets as a percentage of loans continue to climb and reached 7.6% in the third quarter. This is uncomfortably high, especially against a backdrop of an allowance for credit losses that is only one third that size.

NewtekOne is going to need to sacrifice earnings to support cleaning up its loans in future quarters. This makes the risk to the common shares too high to advocate for a buy rating. The preferred shares, with a yield of over 9%, are priced for the risk, but only at a premium of just over 100 basis points compared to the baby bonds. Baby bonds only become impaired if the bank fails, therefore I believe they are the safest investment and despite the challenges, the bank's risk of failure still remains low.

Conclusion

NewtekOne is still transitioning itself from a BDC to a depositor bank, which is creating fluctuations in its performance data. I'm expecting this process to remain volatile through the end of next year. Despite the challenges, I believe the company's baby bonds are a good option for income investors and since NEWTG is offering the highest yield to maturity, that's where I'm recommending investors add new money.

data.title: loanDepot: One Winner From Mortgage-Spread Compression
data.description:
Introduction & Thesis

On December 31, 2025, loanDepot, Inc. (LDI) shares were trading at $2. As readers can see in the image below, the stock reached $3.14 last Friday, before settling at $2.91 at current levels. This represents a 37% increase from the local high and a 32% increase from its current price. Anyone who reads the financial press regularly will have read that this explosion in the share price has not been a local phenomenon, occurring only in this company, but rather across the entire industry associated with the US housing market.

Last week, Donald Trump said he was paying attention to the American housing market. He confirmed this intention when he “asked” government-sponsored enterprises (GSEs) such as Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to purchase $200 billion in mortgage bonds. The aim of this mortgage bond purchase is to ease access to the mortgage market, achieving the following effects.

When GSEs make that purchase of mortgage bonds (due to excess cash), they will narrow the mortgage spread, which is the difference between the average 30-year mortgage and the 10-year Treasury bond (see image below). This occurs because the price of MBS rises and their yield falls. If the yield on MBSs decreases, mortgage originators will be able to offer cheaper average mortgages, thus complying with the Trump administration's wishes. Basically, what they are trying to do is intervene in the housing market by pushing up the prices of mortgage-backed securities (MBSs) to lower the average cost per mortgage.

This is a clear catalyst for loanDepot, as it is a non-bank mortgage lender. As the mortgage spread narrows, mortgages fall and affordability rises, resulting in more transactions that benefit companies of this type in terms of revenue. There may also be a boom in mortgage refinancing, which would increase the number of transactions carried out by these types of companies, positively impacting their income statements. For all these reasons, my thesis today is to tactically buy this stock.

The Business Model & Operating Segments: How loanDepot Makes Money

loanDepot's business model is very easy to understand. Acting as a real estate servicer, its value proposition is organized into five layers: home purchase, home refinance, home equity, home search, and personal loans. It is a kind of 360º service that covers both buyer acquisition and the execution of the home purchase, as well as the post-closing relationship and future refinancing, if any.

As we mentioned, if the GSEs engage in massive purchases of MBS and those purchases succeed in narrowing the mortgage spread between the average 30-year mortgage and the 10-year bond, this will likely trigger higher volume in the Purchase and Refinancing business lines, as well as improved customer recapture in servicing products (search, personal loans). In my view, it is this combination that the market is valuing to position this stock with growth of over 30% this week.

Unit Economics: loanDepot Status

By focusing on the main unit economics of the business, we can see whether they support the argument put forward in the introductory section of the article. As can be seen in the image above, market share remains fairly constant throughout the different quarters analyzed, reaching a maximum of 1.5% and a minimum of 1.2%. Although market share declined in the last quarter (Q3 2025), we can see that the gross margin line expressed in bps has increased to reach the second-highest score (339) across the entire spectrum analyzed. If we also look at block and origination volumes (green and red bars), we see that in this last period it has also reached local highs, with $7 trillion and $6.5 trillion, respectively. What these numbers tell us is that if the aforementioned compression occurs, not only will origination and block volumes improve, but the purchase mix will also shift, giving greater weight to products with higher business margins.

From a cost structure perspective, we can also draw some clear conclusions. In its Q3 2025 corporate presentation, the company separates volume-related expenses (commissions and marketing) from non-volume-related expenses (salaries, other interest, and other G&A). In addition, it superimposes a line of FTEs that drops from 4,815 to 4,509, achieving proper stabilization. Looking ahead and to confirm that there is an upturn in business origination, we should see the variable cost portion increase (volume related), without fixed costs increasing too much (which appear to have a constant structure). If that happens, the increase in revenue from higher origination will improve the company's margin, benefiting EBITDA and operating cash flow. This would certainly be very positive for the share price.

The chart above shows a slightly growing servicing portfolio (UPB), which goes from USD 115 trillion to USD 118 trillion between Q3 2024 and Q3 2025. It can also be seen how the valuation of the MSR (Mortgage Servicing Right) remains relatively stable, with a high mortgage spread. At the bottom are the recapture and retention metrics, which indicate the percentage of customers who repeat and are retained by the company. These will be key in the scenario outlined above, as refinancing will also increase as the mortgage spread narrows and originations increase. The latter will put downward pressure on the company's MSR value, as it will no longer charge for the MSR on old mortgages that are refinanced. This is where recapture and retention ratios come in as key variables, as the company will need to retain these customers so that they return to use its products, thereby marginally increasing its profits.

Final Thoughts

Last but not least, we have the company's P&L. Adjusted Total Revenue rose from $291 million to $325 million in the third quarter of 2025. Adjusted EBITDA also doubled in the same period, indicating growth and strengthening market share. The downside is that the company continues to post net losses in the last two periods (8.7 in the most recent). This tells me a little about the fragility of its profits. Looking ahead to the coming quarters, if the compression of the mortgage spread translates into more originations or better secondary market performance, this operating leverage (increased revenue with an already adjusted cost base) could support an additional re-rating. Thank you for reading.

data.title: ReposiTrak: Better Business, Fairer Price, Still Not A Buy
data.description:
Thesis

ReposiTrak, Inc. (TRAK) is a microcap SaaS (software-as-a-service) company. It provides technology that helps retailers and suppliers follow supply chain rules, improve food safety, and track products through the supply chain. Most of the money comes in on repeat subscriptions, and the margins look respectable enough.

The last time I checked in on TRAK, I had it marked as a Hold. Nothing was on fire. The business looked fine. No argument there. That said, the valuation was stretched, with the P/E sitting north of 60. At the same time, operating costs were creeping higher. And on top of that, adoption leaned heavily on FDA decisions.

Since then, that cautious view has proven correct. TRAK is down about 36% from my last call, while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up more than 21% over the same period. That difference is significant and disappointing.

After reviewing the latest data and fundamentals, I’m still neutral on the stock. The company is doing its job and the valuation has come back to earth, but the ceiling isn’t very high.

Growth, Efficiency, and Financial Leverage

First fiscal quarter revenue increased 10% from $5.4 million to $6 million.

Based on last quarter’s results, ReposiTrak increased its revenue by focusing on recurring sources of income. This growth was driven by selling additional traceability, compliance, and supply chain services to existing customers. This really comes down to using one unified platform. Once a customer plugs in, it’s easy for them to adopt. And because the company already has those relationships, it can step in fast and solve problems such as data errors in complex supply chains.

Management explains that demand for traceability first grew because of government regulations, but it's now being driven even more by retailer requirements. Big retailers like Walmart (WMT) and Kroger (KR) are tightening the rules, in fact, they’re going further than what the federal government requires, so if you’re a supplier, you don’t really have a choice. You have to comply earlier, because otherwise you risk falling behind competitors.

  • Total operating expenses for the quarter increased 3%.
  • SG&A costs were up 6%.

As demand keeps picking up and customers stick around in greater numbers, the company's started spending money to keep the whole thing from buckling under its own growth. Ergo, operating expenses ticked up a bit. That’s mostly because they put money into improving the traceability network, building automated onboarding tools, and beefing up cybersecurity. The focus is on smaller suppliers, especially since they usually don’t have great IT systems and often struggle with clean, accurate data. Automation helps fix these high error rates, which improves day-to-day operations without needing to hire a proportional number of additional employees.

That said, this cost shift brings back a worry I flagged in my last piece. In a few areas, costs are growing faster than revenue, which could pinch margins if adoption cools off. Most of the SG&A increase comes from payroll changes tied to higher revenue, plus higher insurance costs, so yes, the model still looks lean, but it’s clearly feeling the pressure from industry-wide inflation.

  • [ReposiTrak] grew total revenue at approximately twice the rate of SG&A expenses and at 3x the rate of total operating expense growth.
  • Income from operations was up 28% to $1.9 million versus $1.5 million.
  • Simultaneously, [ReposiTrak] delivered $356,000 of revenue per employee on an annualized basis, twice the rate of the 2024 statistic software industry average of $175,000 per employee.
  • Historically, the company had net operating losses to offset income for income tax purposes, resulting in an effective tax rate of approximately 4% to 6%.

Profitability improved because revenue grew faster than expenses. Automation also helped by increasing revenue per employee through better error detection and more efficient, streamlined processes. Still, that drop-through to net income got weaker because the net operating losses rolled off. As a result, the effective tax rate stepped up. Management knows this is structural, not a one-off. They say they can offset some of it with tax credits.

  • Cash from operations was $1.5 million, down from $1.9 million in the year ago quarter.
  • Total cash increased to $28.8 million from $28.6 million at June 30, and the company continues to have 0 bank debt.

Even so, it likely drags a bit on cash efficiency. Cash from operations dipped, but mainly because deferred revenue rolled into reported income. So it’s a timing thing, not a demand issue. Meanwhile, liquidity still looks solid. Cash keeps building, there’s no debt, and that gives TRAK plenty of room to return capital.

Network-Driven Competitive Moat

We have some key competitive advantages in the area. And I think over time, those advantages will create an even bigger moat between us and any alternatives that might arise…If a customer is using the RTN, he’s already done the hard work of connecting. Data has been collected, synchronized, scrubbed and mapped. As a result, expanding into other of our services, such as compliance or supply chain becomes relatively easy. - CEO Fields.

Basically, the segments are growing at a pretty even clip. That’s mostly because traceability has a real moat. It benefits from network scale, and the company is good at cleaning messy data, which then pulls customers into compliance products as well. Because everything sits on the same data backbone, cross-selling works naturally. Keep in mind, companies such as FoodLogiQ and OpsSmart offer similar FSMA tools, which is dividing market share among multiple providers.

We are increasingly being seen as the de facto choice to address the ever-changing food industries need for compliance, traceability and supply chain work.....Errors in the supply chain for traceability create an enormous problem in correcting errors. They don’t show up in one place. They typically show up in 4, 5 or 6 places, and each of those is a different entity, a different business. - CEO Fields.

All of that works best in a world where customers are forced to move on schedule. The recent decision by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration to extend enforcement of FSMA 204 until July 2028 reduces the short-term pressure to comply with the rule. This may cause some suppliers to delay onboarding, which could slow ReposiTrak’s growth if demand from retailers does not fully make up for it. Macro supply chains are another reason to be wary. Geopolitics and economic whiplash are pushing companies to scatter operations, and every extra handoff is another place traceability quietly breaks.

Also, the company's betting on a code overhaul, shifting people and money toward baking in, no surprise here whatsoever, artificial intelligence, to speed development and cut down on human involvement. Management's pitch is that this is the right moment, thanks to solid cash flow. This might be over-worrying, but a risk is that pulling developers off existing work could slow near-term progress if the whole thing becomes a distraction.

TRAK's Valuation

TRAK's currently trading at a blended P/E ratio of 30.83x, much lower than the 60s level I flagged a while back. It's a valuation that assumes 10%+ revenue growth and higher profit margins driven by automation.

Even so, future expectations are somewhat more cautious. Analysts project EPS growth of 11.43% for June 2026, followed by a much larger increase to 30.77% in 2027, telling us the stock already reflects slower growth after the FDA extension.

Fair value comes in around $12.94, while the stock’s trading at about $11.62. So there’s some upside here, although it’s not huge, assuming efficiencies remain intact, even as tax normalization reduces cash flow.

No cash burn here, with reserves building. Thin peer data leaves the P/E hanging in midair. A projected 40.35% annual return assumes everything works perfectly. At this price, it won’t take much competition to knock the shine off fast.

Final Takeaway

Rating: Hold. The risk/reward balance is only slightly positive. The company's scheduled to report post-market earnings next month, with an estimated GAAP EPS of $0.09 and revenue of $5.77 million. Until then, there isn’t much upside, but there is meaningful downside if retailer demand slows after the FSMA 204 enforcement delay, or if the code overhaul takes management’s attention away from running the business well. I would only buy more if you already own the stock, and even then, only small amounts on price weakness.

data.title: EPAM Systems: Navigating Post-Bubble Meltdown With Covered Calls
data.description:
Our recent trade in EPAM Systems Inc. (EPAM) is one that hits many different facets of our investing process. We will go over this company today from that lens and tell you what trade we placed in 2025.

The Company

EPAM describes its business in the following way.

We are consultants, designers, engineers, architects and trainers who enable our clients to be competitive and disruptive in the marketplace through innovative technology solutions, while helping them to successfully navigate through multiple waves of technology and social change

Source: EPAM

To expand on that, we can add that it operates at the intersection of 3 main areas.

1) Engineering: Building scalable, cloud-native software products, platforms, and complex enterprise systems.

2) Consulting: Providing business strategy, experience design, and technology consulting to help companies innovate.

3) Data & AI: Developing advanced data platforms and AI-driven solutions.

With all that said, let's focus on the different facets of our investing process that led us to EPA.

Technology Is Usually Not Our Bread & Butter

We have generally been weary of the technology sector at the current valuations. Even outside the recent extreme, technology is not one where we spend a lot of time. Our focus is always on income generation using defensive entry points and technology companies don't always enter into that arena. We prefer a solid dividend yield from the company and most technology companies would rather spend that cash on growth. But EPAM was different in a few ways and that got us to monitoring it as it got slammed in 2025.

Valuation Over All Else

Yes, we generally don't go into technology but there was something just majestically poetic about seeing a bubble fully implode. We couldn't take our eyes off it. EPAM was down close to 80% (when we made the trade), versus the Invesco QQQ Trust Inc. (QQQ) being up 59%. Sure, we are measuring from the absolute peak in EPAM price near January 1, 2022, but it must have been still extremely painful for any EPAM holder, whenever they bought in.

That of course is just price. What happened to the company's revenues? They grew by 41%. Investors tend to look at us with disbelief when we make claims that stocks can drop 70%-80% with a strong rise of revenues, simply because their valuations are so horrible. EPAM is a textbook case of that.

Of course that revenue growth combined with the big drop in price over this timeframe, worked wonders for EPAM's price to sales ratio.

To show our real time perspective, here is EPAM's price to sales ratio the day we went long.

It is not like EPAM's profits were impacted anywhere in this timeframe. EV to EBITDA had dropped from 60X to around 12X on September 30, 2025. Today it is still a very reasonable 16X.

In a nutshell, EPAM had some issues with the Russian invasion of Ukraine which displaced some of its workforce. It also slowed its growth rate. When you pay 12X sales, it is a matter of time before something comes and slaps you in the face. Any surprise can be a negative surprise. The last drop in 2025 was created with the thought process that LLMs can replace all consultancies and EPAM's business is on the endangered species list. We did not think that was the case and we also felt, EPAM had the muscle to fight any challenges. Alongside that, it had a rock-solid balance sheet with current assets exceeding total liabilities by $1.4 billion.

That cash is very relevant for a firm of this size.

What Got Us To Buy

EPAM was cheapish, at $151 per share. A lot of bulls might say it was the best thing since sliced bread as you can't get a growing technology company at under 13X forward earnings. This brings us to another problem we have with technology companies. They really abuse the stock based compensation model and EPAM was no different. You can see just all the addbacks to GAAP earnings that get us to the adjusted number. $3.13 of stock based compensation per share (the next two lines are just a breakdown of that).

There are some other big addbacks that don't really make us happy. The way we saw it was that after subtracting the net cash, we were getting the stock at about 17X real earnings.

Not exactly a bargain, but perhaps a bargain for the technology sector. The company was also supporting its stock price with a vigor for the first time.

EPAM had also become a crowded short trade (at least relative to its history).

All of that got us, "if we believe AI is a bubble, then EPAM is likely underpriced because of the fear". But even at $151.00, net of the cash, we still felt the margin of safety was low. Sure, we could add a fair value much higher up, but stocks do get drastically undervalued at the end of bubbles. Look at PayPal (PYPL) trading at single digit forward earnings as an example. Fortunately for us, we have the most important tool in the toolkit. Long dated calls. Unlike the blind, front-month, covered call selling that most buy-write funds do, we tend to look at the long end of the curve. In this case EPAM's longest dated calls that were available were for December 2026. The premiums were stupidly high and we were getting a 23.25% annualized return, if EPAM was just flat over 15 months.

It also got our net cash outlay to $117.00 per share and if you adjust that for the net cash held, you are looking at about $100 per share for the business. That we could live with.

Verdict

As it stands, EPAM has jumped 40% from our entry point. The company has asserted stronger growth ahead and the bears are throwing in the towel. A straight buy would have obviously been the better choice with perfect hindsight. But we are never ready to compromise on valuation and entry points. We are long the covered call setup and plan to hang on to see how this evolves in 2026.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: Earnings Show Lower Interest Rates Helping Bank (Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) reported its fourth quarter earnings. The nation’s largest bank by loans and deposits, JPMorgan Chase is a bellwether that will set the tone for bank earnings across the industry. I last discussed the bank from the perspective of its preferred shares back in July. Today, I believe JPMorgan Chase has benefited from lower interest rates and will continue to do so throughout 2026, making the bank’s shares a Buy.

How Have Interest Rate Trends Affected the Bank?

Lower interest rates have had a stimulating effect on JPMorgan Chase. While the bank’s asset yield resumed its decline in the fourth quarter (dropping 10 basis points to 4.95%), the bank’s borrowing yield also declined by a more accelerated 19 basis points, to 2.94%. This allowed the net interest spread and the net yield to increase by 9 basis points. The net yield, which is a broader measure, now sits at 2.54%, and has increased for two consecutive quarters, reversing a general decline that started in the fourth quarter of 2023.

Just as the yields have trended, so has interest income and expense. Interest income in the fourth quarter fell by $600 million to $48.8 billion, marking the first quarterly decline since the first quarter. Interest expense also fell, and by more than interest income at over $1.5 billion to $23.8 billion. This was also the first quarter to quarter decline in interest expense since the first quarter.

The Federal Reserve’s continuation of interest rate declines is certainly an influence to the bank’s changes in interest income and expense. Fortunately, the increased drop in interest expense has allowed net interest income (interest income less interest expense) to rise by $1 billion to just under $25 billion for the quarter. This is the highest recorded level of net interest income and $1.6 billion higher than in the fourth quarter a year ago.

Changes in Loans and Deposits

The growth in JPMorgan Chase’s balance sheet has allowed it to grow net interest income while simultaneously experiencing lower interest rates. In the fourth quarter, the bank experienced loan growth of 4.1%, the second largest quarterly growth level since the acquisition of First Republic Bank. On a year-over-year basis, loans grew by just under 11%.

The deposit side of the balance sheet was slightly tamer but still had growth. Deposits grew by 0.4% during the fourth quarter and by 6.4% on a year over year basis. While quarterly deposit growth was slow, deposit growth for the year outpaced the commercial banking industry by more than 200 basis points.

For any bank, when loan growth outpaces deposit growth, it means that the loan to deposit ratio is rising. The loan to deposit ratio is important because it signals how much of the bank’s deposits are being used to finance loans. If the ratio is high, the bank may need to rely on external financing, which can increase interest expense and put pressure on net interest income.

For JPMorgan Chase, the loan to deposit ratio is now at the highest level the bank has seen since 2019. Fortunately, at 57%, the ratio is below industry benchmarks and still reasonable. The bank’s external financing, which is influenced by its investment bank, did ease back in the fourth quarter, dropping by $122 billion to $942 billion. This was something I had become uncomfortable about earlier in the year, but the reduction of external financing should continue to provide a boost to net interest income in future quarters.

Risks to JPMorgan Chase

As with any bank, loan performance is the primary risk for investors to monitor. In the case of JPMorgan Chase, nonperforming assets (NPA) steadily rose throughout 2025, but declined by $300 million to $10.3 billion in the fourth quarter. The bank has an allowance for credit losses designed to protect earnings from loan losses. As of the end of the fourth quarter, the allowance for credit losses (1.73%) stood more than 100 basis points higher than the NPA ratio (0.69%), providing adequate coverage.

One other risk that should be monitored by investors throughout 2026 is interest rate risk. While the bank has benefited from rates declining, a sudden reversal in Fed policy and/or increase in short-term rates could have a punitive effect on net interest income. This is since out of the $942 billion in external financing, $442 billion is tied to short-term repo agreements and an additional $64 billion is in short-term borrowings. For the time being, the Fed is projecting lower interest rates, but this risk should be on the mind of investors if inflation re-emergences as a problem.

Conclusion

JPMorgan Chase is certainly capitalizing on the decline in interest rates. The bank has improved its net interest income and has reduced its external financing. JPM continues to maintain loan growth above the industry average and with declining nonperforming assets, profitability should continue to improve. Based on these improved performance measures and solid outlook, I believe JPMorgan shares would be a great addition to investors portfolio in 2026.

data.title: Bank of Montreal: Looking Well-Placed Headed Into 2026
data.description:
Diversification has been a nice crutch for Bank of Montreal (BMO) (BMO:CA) of late. While fiscal 2025 was a more pedestrian year for its bread-and-butter Canadian business, better trends in the Capital Markets, Wealth Management, and U.S. Banking segments, plus some help from buybacks, ultimately drove earnings per share around 25% higher. As you might expect, that's been great for the stock, which has returned nearly 30% since my opening piece last summer.

This is a challenging call. On the one hand, BMO has seen a clear degree of valuation uplift since I first wrote on it, so the shares haven't gotten intrinsically cheaper. On the other hand, BMO has lagged its domestic peers in that time, so you could argue the relative investment case has actually improved. While I will keep BMO as a “watch list” stock for now, readers with existing exposure to Canadian banks may consider this a name to rotate into.

Diversification And Capital Helped In 2025…

(All financial figures are in Canadian dollars unless stated otherwise)

Last year was a tumultuous one for the Canadian economy, and I doubt readers would be surprised to learn that BMO's domestic credit charges rose sharply. The bank set aside $2.36 billion to cover potential bad debt in its Canadian Personal and Commercial Banking segment last year, 42% more than in fiscal 2024. While the situation was healthier above this line (pre-provision income rose 7% last year), it wasn't quite enough to lift that unit's adjusted net income, which fell 4% to $3.35 billion. At 19.8%, the adjusted return on was still very healthy, though it was down 170 basis points compared to 2024.

This is where being a universal bank has its benefits. While BMO's domestic engine spluttered a little last year, its U.S. Banking, Wealth Management, and Capital Markets segments all delivered strong double-digit earnings growth. All in, BMO did $9.25 billion in adjusted net profit last year, up 24% on 2024. Buybacks kicked in an extra 145 basis points in terms of share-level growth, as the bank directed some of its excess capital back into investors' pockets.

… And Could Again In 2026

Clearly, 25% annual earnings growth will not be the long-term norm for BMO. That said, I still see more going right than wrong for the bank in 2026.

Let's start with the all-important Canadian P&C segment. Last year, provisioning on impaired loans ran at 58 basis points relative to total loans in this business, the highest print in years. Household mortgages account for around 45% of this unit's loans. While there isn't much loss content in that part of the book, BMO has had to set aside more money to cover things like credit card and business lending.

Now, gross impaired loans have continued to tick up throughout this period. 0.86% of BMO's Canadian loans were impaired at the end of its fourth quarter, up from 0.64% at the start of the year.

Positively, some macro trends have started to brighten up since the end of that period—including within the Canadian jobs report, with Canada adding nearly 130,000 jobs in the last three months of the calendar year, more than across the whole of the first nine months. Meanwhile, at 6.8%, the county's unemployment rate is around 30 basis points off last year's peak. Last year, higher provisioning in the Canadian P&C business was a $705 million year-on-year pre-tax headwind to BMO. If that line can simply stabilize in 2026, it'll help.

That's important because there are tailwinds elsewhere in BMO's business. Ironically, it's the weakest part of the group that I am most bullish about: the U.S. Banking segment. That business chipped in roughly $3.1 billion in net income last year, 34% more than in 2024. In contrast to the Canadian P&C segment, U.S. Banking's credit cycle probably bottomed in BMO's fiscal 2024. Credit charges were $1.68 billion in that year, falling to $1.04 billion in 2025. Sequential trends have also been favorable, with credit expenses falling for four straight quarters.

Despite this, U.S. Banking's profitability remains weak. This unit's adjusted return on equity was only 8.1% last year, nearly 400 basis points below management's 12% target. However, given improving credit costs, 2025's earnings and ROE were weighted more toward the last two quarters. Assuming a full year of cleaner credit in fiscal 2026, that business could be a source of low-hanging fruit for BMO.

The outlook for some of BMO's non-banking businesses looks decent as well. For instance, in addition to managing and/or administering the assets of its wealth management clients, BMO is also one of the largest ETF providers in Canada. It also owns InvestorLine, a self-directed broker for retail investors. In total, BMO currently has $390 billion in assets under management and $283 billion in assets under administration in its Wealth segment.

As you can imagine, a chunk of these assets are in equity index trackers. Buoyant financial markets have been good for this business, lifting asset-based fees. Last year, BMO booked just over $1 billion in net profit in its Wealth and Asset Management subdivision, nearly 30% more than in 2024. Positively, markets seem to have gotten off to a decent start in the firm's fiscal 2026, which should help support asset levels and fees.

Then there's the capital position to consider. BMO ended last year with a 13.3% ratio of Common Equity Tier 1 to risk-weighted assets, 80 basis points above management's target. Given it had $438 billion in RWAs, this would imply around $3.5 billion in surplus capital, equal to nearly 3% of BMO's current market cap.

This cushion is allowing BMO to supplement its regular distributions with buybacks. In addition to paying out over 50% of last year's profits via dividends, it also retired 3% of its outstanding shares. The bank is now earning a healthy 2%-plus return on its risk-weighted assets, giving it plenty of scope to keep topping up dividends with buybacks, fund organic growth, and maintain its capital ratio near management's target. The buyback element will naturally contribute positively to EPS growth.

Valuation

Given the above, I do think BMO is well placed heading into 2026. However, valuation matters in investing, and this is where I think things get a little more challenging, as the stock has gained over 25% since my initial piece. Back then, BMO was trading for 1.37 times its book value—which was in line with its ten-year average. This has since risen to 1.67 times.

BMO's dividend yield has been on a similar journey. It was 4.4% when I first covered the stock, also in line with its ten-year average. It has since fallen to 3.6%.

While the above would suggest that BMO is not intrinsically cheap, it is important to point out that the stock has actually lagged its peers recently. Indeed, while BMO has seen a 22% uplift in its price-to-book ratio since my opening piece, Royal Bank of Canada (RY), Toronto-Dominion (TD), Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce (CM), and Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS) have seen 28%-32% moves in their respective multiples in that time. That being the case, investors with existing exposure to Canadian banks may wish to consider rotating into BMO, though I remain “Neutral” for now.

data.title: Franklin Small Cap Growth Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: US equities delivered solid gains over the fourth quarter of 2025, maintaining their upward trajectory against a backdrop of generally robust corporate earnings, despite some mixed economic signals and investor concerns about stretched valuations in technology-related stocks. Continued monetary easing by the US Federal Reserve (Fed), along with a constructive shift in US–China relations, also underpinned investor sentiment. The outperformance of the Magnificent Seven mega-capitalization technology stocks during the quarter boosted returns for the S&P 500 Index and the Nasdaq Composite Index, while the relatively strong performance of the Dow Jones Industrial Average reflected the enduring strength of blue-chip companies. By investment style, value investing surpassed growth in the large-, mid- and small-capitalization tiers, with large-cap stocks performing better than their mid- and small-cap counterparts.
  • Contributors: For the quarter, stock selection decisions factored into the fund’s relative outperformance. Strong stock selection bolstered relative results in the information technology (IT) sector. Stock selection and an underweight in the industrials sector and stock selection in the energy sector also contributed to a lesser extent.
  • Detractors: Stock selection and an overweight in the consumer staples sector detracted most from relative performance in the fourth quarter. An underweight in the health care sector and stock selection and an underweight in the real estate sector also proved adverse for relative returns.
  • Outlook: The performance of small-cap stocks in general was positive for the fourth quarter and for 2025, supported by broadly favorable valuations, earnings growth and monetary policy. Future performance is likely to depend on the health of the US economy, Fed decisions and overall investor confidence. We believe small-cap growth stocks are attractively valued, both relative to large caps and in light of their own underlying growth potential.

Top Equity Issuers (% of Total)

Holding

Fund

BWX TECHNOLOGIES INC (BWXT)

2.93

ARCOSA INC (ACA)

2.72

AAR CORP (AIR)

2.34

2.14

2.09

1.99

LUMENTUM HOLDINGS INC (LITE)

1.97

SITIME CORP (SITM)

1.97

Performance Review

  • The fund’s return (Advisor Class without sales charges) was positive and meaningfully outperformed that of the Russell 2000 Growth Index. In the IT sector, Lumentum Holdings was a top contributor to returns. The photonics specialist reported solid fiscal first-quarter 2026 financial results, but it was the company’s second-quarter guidance of more than 20% sequential growth that rallied the stock. Increased artificial intelligence (AI) demand benefited its core business, including transceiver components and data center interconnects.
  • Olema Pharmaceuticals (OLMA) was a leading contributor in the health care sector. Shares of the clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company jumped after a competitor’s breast cancer drug trial succeeded, boosting confidence in Olema’s similar therapy. The company is a developer of targeted treatments for breast cancer.
  • In contrast, the health care sector was hindered by a position in uniQure (QURE) (not held by period-end), whose shares fell sharply after the US Food & Drug Administration withdrew its support for the firm’s Huntington’s disease trial data, raising regulatory uncertainty for the biotechnology company.

Outlook

  • As we enter 2026, our view on US equities remains positive but selective. In an environment where growth is available but not abundant, our focus is on owning high-quality franchises with durable competitive advantages, often supported by secular tailwinds.
  • In recent years, a small group of mega-capitalization innovators—dominated by the Magnificent Seven—has driven the majority of index returns, benefiting from scale and AI leadership. Looking toward 2026, we believe conditions should favor a broadening of equity market leadership, with opportunities emerging across sectors beyond IT and companies below the mega-cap threshold, which can offer an advantage for small- and mid-cap companies.
  • In 2026, we are focused on structural themes shaped by AI and automation, which are now producing measurable productivity gains not only in the IT sector but also in the health care, financials, industrials and energy sectors. We are also monitoring potential risks for the US economy, including inflation, regulatory pressures, geopolitical friction, infrastructure bottlenecks and unfavorable policy shifts.
  • Fiscal and economic conditions appear to be aligning to support growth. We expect continued innovation-led expansion underpinned by quality fundamentals in an environment where we think disciplined, active management has an advantage.

Benchmark(s)

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 5/1/2000. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (a) for Advisor Class periods prior to 5/1/2000, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (b) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

The total annual operating expenses are as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. Small- and mid-cap stocks involve greater risks and volatility than large-cap stocks. The investment style may become out of favor, which may have a negative impact on performance. To the extent the portfolio invests in a concentration of certain securities, regions or industries, it is subject to increased volatility. Liquidity risk exists when securities or other investments become more difficult to sell, or are unable to be sold, at the price at which they have been valued. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) is an unmanaged index composed of 30 blue-chip stocks, each with annual sales exceeding $7 billion. The DJIA is price-weighted, reflects large-cap companies representative of U.S. industry, and historically has moved in tandem with other major market indexes such as the S&P 500. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC.

The Magnificent Seven stocks are Apple, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, Nvidia, and Tesla.

Nasdaq Composite Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index that is designed to represent the performance of Nasdaq securities and includes over 3,000 stocks. Source: Nasdaq OMX.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. The Russell 2000 Growth Index measures the performance of the small-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change. Source: FTSE. Source: © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved.

Holdings are provided for informational purposes only and should not be construed as a recommendation to purchase or sell any security. Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

© Franklin Templeton. All rights reserved.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.

Not FDIC Insured | No Bank Guarantee | May Lose Value

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Apple: The Gemini Move May Lead To iPhone Upgrades And A New AI Distribution Model
data.description:
If strong iPhone 17 sales, despite no major Apple Intelligence upgrades, wasn't enough for the bulls on Apple Inc. (AAPL), the multiyear partnership to integrate Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOGL) Google Gemini into a revamped Siri later this year adds a second, more durable catalyst to the narrative.

I see two key revenue drivers from this partnership.

First, Apple Intelligence is still limited to iPhone 15 Pro and iPhone 16 models or newer, leaving a large portion of the installed base unable to access the feature set. If Gemini materially improves Siri’s usefulness, I see upside from a major upgrade cycle ahead.

That said, the momentum of the iPhone 17 makes me believe that users don't really care that much about AI features. This is a risk, given that Apple will be spending $1B a year for the Gemini integration.

The second pillar is related to AI distribution. As model providers improve monetization, Apple can replicate the default-search playbook by charging for default assistant/model placement. Given the September remedies ruling in the Google antitrust case, I believe there will be multiple credible bidders (Gemini, OpenAI (OPENAI), Anthropic (ANTHRO)) engaging in bidding wars.

At roughly 31x forward earnings after a 10% pullback, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating on Apple.

Could A Gemini-Powered Siri Become A 2026 iPhone Catalyst?

Let's start with the headlines.

Apple announced yesterday a multiyear partnership to integrate Google Gemini AI into a revamped Siri later this year. Unfortunately no concrete timeline (H1 vs. H2) was disclosed. I am not surprised in the slightest given the delays with Siri last year and, based on price action (Apple stock was relatively flat on Monday), I believe the market hasn't blinked either.

Furthermore, Google said Apple chose Gemini as the foundation for Apple’s next-gen foundation models, which will also power future Apple Intelligence features. I have a high conviction that the ChatGPT integration into Siri (which is an opt-in for complex queries since the end of 2024) will be replaced with Gemini. What does it mean for Google?

Well, as I said in my last Google coverage, a Gemini - ChatGPT crossover point is likely to happen this year (especially after Monday's partnership announcement):

Assuming Gemini maintains its 30% MAU growth rate (the same as in the period between August and November 2025) and OpenAI (OPENAI) its 5%-6% growth rate, I estimate the crossover point could be in roughly 12 months.

However, I'm here to talk about Apple. So, what's the upside for the iPhone maker?

First, Apple reiterated a key aspect of its architecture: Apple Intelligence will be designed to run on-device where possible. The Samsung Galaxy S25 family (in my view, iPhone's top competitor) is able to run Galaxy AI on-device. From a privacy perspective, this is a key feature. Now, for more complex tasks, Apple Intelligence will use Private Cloud Compute for more complex requests while still preserving the user's privacy. In other words, a Gemini-powered Siri does not automatically mean Google controls user data.

Never underestimate the value of privacy. In an age when every letter you type on a keyboard can be used to train frontier large language models, or LLMs, privacy could be the USP that convinces skeptical consumers (like me) to upgrade to a new phone.

So, what's the price tag for the Gemini's brain?

Neither Apple nor Google disclosed the exact amount. All we have is Bloomberg's whisper figure of $1B a year for a 1.2T parameter model.

How about the upside?

Here is where Apple hit the nail on its head. According to Apple Support, Apple Intelligence is only available for iPhone 15 Pro models and iPhone 16 models or newer.

The chart below is really interesting to understand the upside. It represents the top 10 models of iPhones for each week in the last year (in percentages) from 16M monthly active app users:

Notably, 52% of handset devices represent older models than the iPhone 15 Pro. Assuming 2B active devices, out of which, 1.5B could be iPhones, there could be 750 million iPhone devices that are incompatible with Apple Intelligence.

The upside could materialize as upgrades to newer, more expensive iPhone models. As seen below, on a TTM basis, iPhone revenue growth has been relatively modest over the past few years, despite the (unexpected) success of the iPhone 17.

That is, if (and only if) Gemini materially improves Siri usefulness (accuracy, context, and task completion). According to some sources, Siri handled about 1.5B requests per day in 2024. Even if we assume no growth since then (not a hard assumption, given the lack of upgrades), compared with an installed base of 2B+ active devices (~1.5B iPhones), I don’t think it’s a stretch to say a good chunk of iPhone users rely on Siri as a go-to feature they use daily.

Finally, a better Siri could be a tailwind for the services segment, which desperately needs to return back to mid to high teens of revenue growth.

If Siri becomes more useful, I believe it could increase time spent on handset device, which is historically correlated with consumer spending (and, part of that spending could be a tailwind for the services segment).

Another (Larger) Upside: AI Distribution

In the same way that Apple historically monetized default search placement (by getting paid big dollars to route high-intent user traffic to a search partner), I believe Apple could monetize AI queries.

Apple's moat is its massive installed base of 2B+ users.

The business model is relatively simple: a model provider (Gemini, ChatGPT, Claude, Mistral AI) pays Apple for being the default assistant model. In exchange, the model provider makes revenue from the user request (mainly through ads, although monetizing AI is a fragile topic at the moment).

The September remedies ruling in the Google antitrust case allows Google to keep the Apple deal and continue revenue-sharing payments. The only caveat is that the default-search placement agreements can only run for one year at a time and must be renegotiated/renewed annually. That's bad for Google and great for Apple, given that I expect a similar structure for the default LLM model in iPhone's devices. I count three large LLM providers (Gemini, ChatGPT, Claude) who would likely compete fiercely for being Apple's default LLM provider.

Now, let me clarify something. Yesterday's announcement says that the next generation of Apple foundation models will be based on Google’s Gemini models and cloud technology. That doesn’t imply Apple will start routing AI queries to Gemini (and getting paid for the placement).

At least, not yet. That will be the next leg of the growth story, once the likes of Gemini and ChatGPT figure out how to monetize their LLM chatbots. That’s when I expect bidding wars to break out to become Apple’s default LLM provider.

Valuation

Apple is trading at just 31x next year's earnings. The stock experienced a 10% haircut from the highs last month, and I see a key support level at $260.

Interestingly enough, Apple is trading at the same forward P/E multiple as Alphabet. Compared to the rest of the Mag 7 (ex-Tesla, as price action is not driven by fundamentals), the two stocks are in the #1 and #2 place in terms of the last 6-month returns.

Yet, I don't see their multiple significantly diverging from the Mag 7 group:

Overall, I believe valuation still makes sense, despite the outperformance in the past 6-months.

Risks

First, the unexpected success (see Tim Cook's remarks below) of the iPhone 17 is a double edged sword:

The iPhone 17 has been -- the family has been very well received there. We do believe that we'll return to growth in Q1, and that is largely based on the reception of the iPhone there. And so I couldn't be more pleased with how things are going there in the early going.

To me, the success of the iPhone 17 suggests that users are not concerned with AI features. Apple Intelligence may not be as desirable as the bulls think. If this is the case, any improvements to Siri could end up being a waste if users don't care about AI features.

As I said before, Apple was reported to pay Alphabet approximately $1B a year for the Gemini partnership. If an upgrade driven by better AI doesn't materialize, that billion a year will pressure the bottom line of the company.

The same goes for the AI distribution pillar. If users don't use AI search, Apple doesn't have that much of an edge as the bulls believe.

Overall, I believe the risks mentioned above are fat tail risks. Very small chance, in my view, but high impact.

Conclusion

I reiterate my Strong Buy rating on Apple, especially after the 10% cool off from the December highs.

The key factor to monitor is whether the Siri revamp (planned for later this year) actually ships without further slippage. Any delays could hurt the sentiment around the stock, given that the market is now pricing in several tailwinds from the Gemini partnership.

First, Apple Intelligence will need to deliver clear gains in accuracy, context, and task completion. If the upgrade in intelligence and usefulness is significant, the upside comes from a base of 750M iPhones (approximately 50% of total devices) that are incompatible with Apple Intelligence.

Second, if users adopt the new Apple Intelligence features, I believe Apple could become an AI distribution powerhouse. In the same way the company is charging for default search placements, I believe they will charge for default LLM providers. This could enable a new growth leg, once the LLM providers figure out how to monetize AI.
